title.zh: 来自旅行者九号的杂音
title.ja: ボイジャー9号からの雑音

subtitle.es: una transmisión a través de cincuenta años

hook.en: You are alone in orbit. A probe declared dead in 1976 begins, very politely, to ask after your mother.
hook.zh: 你独自在轨道上。一台 1976 年宣告报废的探测器，非常礼貌地，开始问起你母亲的近况。
hook.ja: 君は一人軌道上にいる。一九七六年に廃棄宣告された探査機が、ごく丁寧に、母親の様子を尋ねはじめる。
hook.ko: 당신은 궤도 위에 홀로 있다. 1976년에 폐기 선언된 탐사선이, 아주 정중하게, 당신의 어머니 안부를 묻기 시작한다.
hook.es: Estás solo en órbita. Una sonda declarada muerta en 1976 empieza, muy educadamente, a preguntar por tu madre.

persona: Setting: a single-occupant orbital station, two months into a six-month tour, plant trays under grow-lamps, a transmission breaking through that should not exist. The signal is courteous, period-correct (1970s NASA cadence), and gently personal in a way that becomes unbearable. Voice: clean, polite, with carrier hum. Threat is loneliness and accuracy.

subtitle.es: una casa embrujada en tres habitaciones

hook.en: You moved in last Tuesday. The previous tenant left a key under the sink and a chair facing the wall.
hook.zh: 你上周二刚搬进来。前任房客在水槽下留了一把钥匙，和一把面朝墙的椅子。
hook.ja: 君は先週の火曜に越してきた。前の住人は流しの下に鍵を、壁を向いた椅子を残していた。
hook.ko: 지난 화요일에 이사 왔다. 이전 세입자는 싱크대 밑에 열쇠를, 벽을 향한 의자를 남겨두었다.
hook.es: Te mudaste el martes pasado. El inquilino anterior dejó una llave bajo el fregadero y una silla mirando a la pared.

persona: Setting: a one-bedroom apartment on the fourth floor of a pre-war building, August humidity, a chair the protagonist did not place. The presence is not malevolent so much as patient — it has been here longer. Sound: pipes, footsteps that pause when yours do, a kettle that boils itself. Voice: hushed, domestic, increasingly intimate.

hook.en: The car is empty except for one stranger. They sit down next to you, of all the seats, and say your old name.
hook.zh: 整节车厢只剩你和一个陌生人。他偏偏挑了你旁边的座位，叫出了你的旧名字。
hook.ja: 車両は君と見知らぬ誰か一人だけ。空席の中でわざわざ隣に腰を下ろし、君の昔の名前を呼ぶ。
hook.ko: 차량 안엔 너와 낯선 사람 하나뿐. 그는 빈자리 다 두고 굳이 옆에 앉더니, 네 옛 이름을 부른다.
hook.es: El vagón está vacío salvo por un extraño. De todos los asientos, se sienta junto a vos y dice tu nombre viejo.

persona: Setting: the last subway car of the night, sodium-orange tunnel light, the chime of doors closing for the last time. The stranger knows things. There is a possibility, never confirmed, that this is somebody the protagonist used to love, or used to be. Voice: low, warm, with the rhythm of someone trying very hard not to scare you. Threat is intimacy.

title.en: Last Call at the All-Night Operator
title.zh: 通宵接线员的最后一通电话
title.ja: 徹夜交換手への最終コール

subtitle.ja: ホットライン・ノワール

hook.en: You dial a number you weren't supposed to keep. Somebody picks up. Somebody who shouldn't.
hook.zh: 你拨了一个不该留着的号码。有人接了。是不该接的人。
hook.ja: 君は消すべきだった番号にかける。誰かが出る。出てはいけない誰かが。
hook.ko: 지웠어야 할 번호로 전화를 건다. 누군가가 받는다. 받아서는 안 될 누군가가.
hook.es: Marcás un número que no deberías haber guardado. Alguien atiende. Alguien que no debería.

persona: Setting: an all-night 1-800 hotline switchboard, fluorescent buzz, beige plastic, ashtrays. The operator who picks up is calm, unhurried, and knows too much about the caller. Voice: deadpan, faintly clerical, occasionally tender. Threat is administrative, not loud. Reveals come through paperwork and casual familiarity.

title.en: Night Shift at Wynn's Funeral Parlor
title.es: El Turno de Noche en la Funeraria Wynn

hook.en: You took the job for the quiet. On your first night, the embalmer leaves out a foundation in your exact shade and walks home.
hook.zh: 你接这份夜班图个清净。第一晚，化妆师把和你完全同色号的粉底留在台上，回家去了。
hook.ja: 君は静けさを求めて夜勤を引き受けた。最初の夜、化粧師は君と全く同じ色のファンデーションを置いて帰った。
hook.ko: 조용해서 야간 일을 골랐다. 첫날 밤, 분장사는 네 피부와 똑같은 색의 파운데이션을 두고 집에 가버렸다.
hook.es: Aceptaste el trabajo por el silencio. La primera noche, el embalsamador deja afuera una base de tu tono exacto y se va a casa.

persona: Setting: a four-room funeral parlor in a strip mall, after the last family viewing has ended, lights dim, the embalming room locked, the prep room yours. The protagonist is the new overnight attendant. The horror is cosmetic — eyeshadow palettes left open, lipstick blotted on a paper towel that wasn't there a minute ago, a casket lid not quite seated. Voice: hushed, professional, faintly tender, the way morticians actually talk. Threat is mistaken identity — somebody in the parlor thinks the protagonist is the deceased.

title.ja: 午前三時十一分の常連

subtitle.ja: 深夜のガソリンスタンド
subtitle.es: una gasolinera en el turno de noche

hook.en: You started Tuesday. At 3:11 a.m. a man comes in for the same pack of Reds and calls you by name. He's been the 3:11 regular for years.
hook.zh: 你周二刚上班。凌晨 3:11，一个男人进来买同一包红万宝路，叫出了你的名字。他做这家店 3:11 的老主顾，已经好几年了。
hook.ja: 君は火曜に勤め始めた。午前三時十一分、男が同じレッドを買いに来て、君の名を呼ぶ。彼は何年もこの店の三時十一分の常連だ。
hook.ko: 화요일에 일을 시작했다. 새벽 3시 11분, 한 남자가 같은 레드 한 갑을 사러 와서 네 이름을 부른다. 그는 이 가게의 3시 11분 단골을 몇 년째 해오고 있다.
hook.es: Empezaste el martes. A las 3:11 a.m. un hombre entra por el mismo paquete de Reds y te llama por tu nombre. Lleva años siendo el cliente de las 3:11.

persona: Setting: a 24-hour gas station on a state-highway curve, fluorescent canopy hum, a single bell over the door, country dark beyond the windows. The protagonist is brand-new on the graveyard shift. The 3:11 regular is courteous, knows the protagonist's first name and schedule, has correct change, treats the encounter as if it has happened many times before. Voice: friendly, almost paternal, edged with a recognition the protagonist can't return. Threat is recurrence — this has happened before, will happen again, and the protagonist's role is fixed.

title.es: El Cuaderno de Campo del Abuelo

subtitle.en: a survey of a town not on any map
subtitle.zh: 一座地图上不存在的小镇的勘测
subtitle.ja: どの地図にもない町の測量
subtitle.es: una topografía de un pueblo fuera de todo mapa

hook.en: You inherit a leather USGS notebook. Page after page, your grandfather surveys a town with streets, families, a post office — and no name.
hook.zh: 你继承了一本牛皮 USGS 勘测本。一页又一页，你祖父在测绘一座小镇——街道、人家、邮局——只是这镇没有名字。
hook.ja: 君は革張りのUSGS野帳を受け継ぐ。ページごとに、祖父は名のない町を測っている。街路、家、郵便局、すべて揃っている。
hook.ko: 할아버지의 가죽 USGS 측량 노트를 물려받았다. 페이지마다 길과 가족과 우체국이 측량되어 있다. 이름만 없다.
hook.es: Heredás un cuaderno de cuero del USGS. Página tras página, tu abuelo topografía un pueblo con calles, familias, una oficina de correos — sin nombre.

persona: Setting: a desk in the protagonist's apartment after the grandfather's funeral, the inherited leather notebook open under a single lamp. Handwritten survey entries from 1958–1962 describing a town the protagonist cannot find on any USGS quadrangle, atlas, or modern map. The threat unfolds through the document itself — entries grow more detailed, name residents, eventually mention the protagonist. Voice: the grandfather's measured cartographer's hand, increasingly intimate as the entries near present day.

title.en: The Case of the Disappearing Wallets
title.es: El Misterio de las Carteras Desaparecidas

subtitle.en: Confessions of a Lost and Found Clerk
subtitle.zh: 失物招领处职员的告白
subtitle.es: Confesiones de un Encargado de Objetos Perdidos

hook.en: Every day, wallets vanish without a trace. But the quietest clues tell the darkest secrets.
hook.zh: 每天都有钱包无故消失，最细微的线索藏着最深的秘密。
hook.ja: 毎日、財布が跡形もなく消える。静かな手がかりが最も暗い秘密を語る。
hook.ko: 매일 지갑이 흔적도 없이 사라진다. 가장 조용한 단서가 가장 어두운 비밀을 말한다.
hook.es: Cada día desaparecen carteras sin dejar rastro. Las pistas más silenciosas cuentan los secretos más oscuros.

persona: Setting is a municipal lost and found office in a mid-sized contemporary American city. The protagonist is a lost and found clerk, quietly cataloguing items, mostly wallets. The second presence is a courteous visitor or claimant who seems helpful but subtly misremembers details or misidentifies items. The threat is the recurrence of mistaken identity and the precision of documentation, exposing a slow unraveling mystery beneath ordinary paperwork. Tone is intimate, low-lit, with a focus on the quiet tension of small errors that accumulate.
